data.title: Dow drops as UnitedHealth Group plunges, S&P, and Nasdaq also slide
data.description:
Major averages extended losses on Thursday, following a tech-led selloff the previous day, as UnitedHealth Group (UNH) plunged after cutting its full-year profit outlook in response to disappointing Q1 2025 results.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.1%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.5%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1.5%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 1 basis point to 4.29%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) dipped 1 basis point to 3.77%.

Wall Street on Wednesday ended deep in the red after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell expressed concerns about the likely higher-than-anticipated effects from tariffs, including possibly putting pressure on the central bank's dual mandate of stable prices and strong employment.

"Ahead of the Easter weekend, risk assets sold off again, as ongoing trade tensions and hawkish comments from Fed Chair Powell led to growing concern about the near-term outlook, particularly if the Fed would be reluctant to ease policy," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"In terms of the latest on the trade front, investors were most concerned about chipmakers yesterday, which followed the new restrictions from the Donald Trump administration on semiconductor exports to China," Allen said.

On the economic front, the initial jobless claims unexpectedly dropped in the past week. 

Housing starts also dropped more than expected in March, and building permits surprised higher.

Markets will be closed tomorrow on account of Good Friday.

data.title: Trump Media asks SEC to look for stock manipulation after Qube's short position
data.description:
Trump Media and Technology (NASDAQ:DJT) sent a letter to the Securities and Exchange Commission on Thursday to look into "suspicious activity" involving the company's stock after Qube Research & Technologies disclosed a short position in the company.

On Monday, a Reuters report, citing a filing with Germany's federal gazette Bundesanzeiger, suggested that the UK-based hedge fund disclosed a nearly $105M short position in the Truth Social parent.

The memo to the SEC asked the regulator to investigate "potential illegal naked short selling and market manipulation of DJT stock."

The company has sent the same memo to the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority, Nasdaq, and the New York Stock Exchange.

Click to read the full memo sent by DJT to the SEC.

data.title: KeyCorp Q1 earnings beat on higher NII; credit loss provision jumps
data.description:
KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY) stock drifted up 2% in Thursday morning trading after turning in Q1 earnings and revenue that rose past the average analyst estimate as the difference between the interest it earns from lending and the interest it pays on deposits climbed during the three-month period. Still, it said aside more capital to cover potential losses from loans at risk of default.

Meanwhile, the regional lender maintained its guidance for full-year 2025. 

Q1 adjusted EPS of $0.38 topping the $0.32 consensus, held steady from the prior quarter and rose from $0.22 a year before.

Total revenue (taxable equivalent) of $1.77B, vs. $1.75B expected, climbed from $865M in Q4 and $1.53B in Q1 2024.

Net interest income ("TE") advanced 4.1% sequentially and 24.7% Y/Y to $1.11B.

Noninterest income was $668M, up from -$196M in Q4 and $647M in the year-earlier quarter.

Trust and investment services income of fell to $139M from $142M in the previous quarter and edged up from $136M in last year's Q1.

Investment banking and debt placement fees were $175M compared with $221M in Q4 and $170M in Q1 2024.

Provision for credit losses jumped to $118M from $39M in Q4 and $101M in Q1 2024.

Average deposits inched up 0.9% Q/Q to $88.3B. Average loans of $104.4B vs. $104.7B in Q4.

Summing up Y/Y trends, Chairman and CEO Chris Gorman said: "Our first quarter results marked a strong beginning to the year. Revenue was up 16% year-over-year while expenses were essentially flat. We achieved both absolute and fee-based positive operating leverage on a year-over-year basis. Sequentially, net interest income grew 4% and the net interest margin increased by 17 basis points to 2.58%."

data.title: Ascent Industries announces multi-year specialty chemicals contract
data.description:
• Ascent Industries (NASDAQ:ACNT) has announced the signing of a significant multi-year contract, expanding its relationship with an existing customer.
  • The agreement is expected to generate more than $0.75M in additional annual EBITDA, representing a 10% year-over-year increase for the Specialty Chemicals segment.
  • “This contract marks a significant milestone in our ongoing transformation and underscores our strategic shift toward becoming a pure-play specialty chemicals company,” said Bryan Kitchen, President and CEO.

data.title: State Street Q1 earnings beat, revenue misses as NII disappoints
data.description:
State Street (NYSE:STT) stock dipped 1.4% in Thursday morning trading after the custodial bank's revenue fell short of Wall Street expectations in the first quarter of 2025, with net interest income coming in softer than expected.

Q1 adjusted EPS of $2.04, topping the average analyst estimate of $2.01, increased/decreased from $2.60 in Q4 2024 and $1.69 in last year's Q1.

Q1 revenue of $3.28B, lagging the $3.32B consensus, fell from $3.41B in the previous quarter and rose from $3.14B a year ago.

Net interest income of $714M, missing the Visible Alpha consensus of $721.8M, fell 5% Q/Q and was flat Y/Y. Net interest margin, on a fully taxable-equivalent basis, was 1.00%, down from 1.07% in the previous quarter and 1.13% a year ago.

Assets under custody or administration were $46.7T vs. $46.6T at Dec. 31, 2024.

Total expenses of $2.45B, less than the Visible Alpha consensus of $2.49B, edged up 0.4% Q/Q and fell 2.5% Y/Y. Excluding notable items, total expenses rose 2.9% Q/Q and 2.8% Y/Y.

Its effective tax rate of 21.7% in Q1 rose from 18.4% in Q4 and declined from 22.5% in Q1 2024.

Earlier, State Street Non-GAAP EPS of $2.04 beats by $0.03, revenue of $3.28B misses by $40M

data.title: Most shorted S&P 500 stocks in March
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) retained the top spot for the most shorted S&P 500 (SP500) stock as a percentage of float in March.

Super Micro shorts raise bets

As of March 31, 22.84% of Super Micro’s (SMCI) shares were sold short, compared to 21.34% at the end of February. Short interest in the artificial intelligence server maker rose to 116.19M shares, marking an increase of 12.8% from Feb-end. SMCI shares have dipped over the past month along with the rest of the market on tariff-related news.

Moderna: Bearish sentiments rise again

Moderna (MRNA) took the second spot, with a short interest of 49.8M shares, representing 14.58% of the float. This marks a 10.5% increase in short interest from the previous month. The biotech has erased 39% in value so far this year and continues to be among the top S&P500 decliners YTD.

Enphase Energy: Short bets ease, market awaits earnings report

Enphase Energy (ENPH) experienced notable fluctuations in its stock performance and short interest last month. The energy tech firm had a short interest of around 18.32M shares at March-end, marking a 23% decline from the previous month. However, shares fell nearly 19% over the past month. Enphase Energy is projected to post its quarterly earnings results after the market closes on April 22. Analysts estimated $0.72 in EPS and revenue of $359.62M for the quarter.

Here are the top 20 most shorted S&P 500 stocks as a percentage of float, as of March:

  • Super Micro Computer (SMCI) - Short Interest as a % of float: 22.84%
  • Moderna (MRNA) - Short Interest as a % of float: 14.58%

data.title: Energy Fuels to expand rare earths production to fill U.S. critical minerals supply gaps
data.description:
Energy Fuels (NYSE:UUUU) +7.6% in early trading Thursday after saying it has developed the technical ability needed to commercially produce rare earth oxides including samarium, gadolinium, dysprosium, terbium, lutetium, and yttrium at scale through expansion of its existing capability at its White Mesa mill in Utah.

The mill currently has the commercial capacity to process monazite ore concentrates into separated NdPr oxide; through ongoing testwork, Energy Fuels (NYSE:UUUU) said it believes it now has the technical knowhow to design, construct, and commission the expansion of its existing infrastructure to produce the six rare earth oxides from monazite "relatively quickly with appropriate U.S. government support and/or market conditions."

Energy Fuels (UUUU) has secured three large-scale heavy mineral sands mines in the Southern Hemisphere, expected to supply monazite concentrates by 2028; the company currently sources monazite from Chemours mines in Florida and Georgia.

The announcement comes as the Trump administration launched a Section 232 investigation on imports of processed critical minerals, including rare earth, uranium, and vanadium oxides produced by the company in the U.S.

data.title: Intel faces potentially significant impact from China's import tariffs: Wells Fargo
data.description:
Intel's (NASDAQ:INTC) efforts to produce semiconductors domestically could significantly affect its revenue from China, which accounted for nearly 30% of its revenue in 2024, according to Wells Fargo.

In response to U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods, China enacted its own 125% tariffs on U.S. products. However, last week the China Semiconductor Industry Association said U.S. firms such as Qualcomm (QCOM) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), who outsource manufacturing to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), are exempted as China regulators classify these chips as originating from Taiwan.

"In contrast, chips made by Intel, Texas Instruments (TXN), ADI (ADI), and ON Semiconductor (ON) - which operate their own U.S.-based fabs - may be classified as U.S. origin and be liable for tariff rates of 84% or higher," the CSIA noted.

Intel has fab production sites located in Arizona, New Mexico and Oregon, as well as Ireland and Israel. It also operates assembly and test sites in Costa Rica, Malaysia, Vietnam and China. Intel is currently building a $28B, 1,000-acre "mega-site" near Columbus, Ohio.

"Consequently, chips fabricated in Taiwan by foundry's like TSMC for U.S. firms, including AMD, NVIDIA, Qualcomm, and Apple (AAPL), are exempt from China's retaliatory tariffs, which could be up to 125%," said Wells Fargo analysts, led by Aaron Rakers, in a Thursday investor note.

"In contrast, wafers produced in the U.S. by manufacturers like Intel are categorized as U.S.-origin products and subject to the tariff," Rakers added. "We expect Intel to see accelerated share erosion within China from this policy."

China made up 27% of Intel's revenue in 2022 and 2023, Wells Fargo noted. It climbed to 29% in 2024.

Wells Fargo rates Intel at Equal Weight with a price target of $25.

Intel is slated to release its first quarter 2025 financial results post-market on Thursday, April 24. A consensus estimate expects Intel to report adjusted earnings per share of zero on revenue of $12.31B.

Intel shares were down 1.6% during early market action on Thursday.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: TSM, LLY, AA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures mixed amid renewed optimism over potential negotiated settlements between the U.S. and key trading partners on tariff-related disputes.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) +5% – Shares advanced after the chipmaker posted mixed Q1 results, with profit surging 60% Y/Y, surpassing expectations on strong demand for AI chips. The company issued upbeat guidance, forecasting Q2 revenue between $28.4B and $29.2B, above the $27.22B consensus. For 2025, TSMC expects revenue growth in the mid-20s% range in U.S. dollar terms. Looking further ahead, management projects a revenue CAGR of nearly 20% from 2024 to 2029, a long-term gross margin of 53% or higher, and a return on equity above 25% through the cycle.
  • MongoDB (NASDAQ:MDB) +2.5% – Shares edged higher after Redburn upgraded the database software provider to Neutral from Sell, citing limited downside risk. Analyst Alexander Haissl noted that MongoDB’s valuation multiple is near all-time lows and earnings expectations have already been reset. While macro-related risks persist, Haissl said they are not specific to the company. The firm set a price target of $170, implying a 6.2% upside from the last close.
  • Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) +16% – Shares soared after its once-daily oral GLP-1 drug, orforglipron, met primary and key secondary endpoints in a Phase 3 trial for type 2 diabetes, showing an average weight loss of ~8% (16 lbs) at the highest dose over 40 weeks. The positive data boosted investor confidence in Lilly’s ability to compete in the obesity drug market, pressuring rival Novo Nordisk (NVO) -8%, while peers Viking (VKTX) and Amgen (AMGN) also declined nearly 4%. Lilly expects to submit regulatory filings by 2025–26, pending further data from ongoing trials.
  • Hertz Global (NASDAQ:HTZ) +28% – Shares surged after Pershing Square disclosed a 4.1% stake in Q1, making it the second-largest shareholder behind Knighthead Capital (58.9%). The move sparked speculation of an activist push, with BofA noting that Bill Ackman’s firm could press for operational changes and board representation. Analyst John Babcock added that the rally may give Hertz flexibility to tap equity markets, as it seeks over $500M in capital. Meanwhile, Morgan Stanley's Adam Jonas highlighted Hertz’s strategic value in an AI-driven future, citing its infrastructure potential in supporting autonomous vehicle networks.

Biggest stock losers

  • Alcoa (NYSE:AA) -3% – Shares slipped after the company posted mixed Q1 results and warned that tariff-related costs are set to rise sharply. Alcoa expects to incur $90M in costs this quarter from U.S. tariffs on aluminum imports from Canada, up from $20M in Q1. CEO William Oplinger noted that about 70% of the company’s Canadian aluminum is shipped to the U.S. and now faces a 25% tariff, weighing on profits by an estimated $100M annually. He added that steep U.S. tariffs on Chinese inputs could further increase annual costs by $10M–$15M due to limited alternative suppliers. While higher domestic aluminum prices may provide some relief, Oplinger said ramping up U.S. production remains impractical in the short term, undercutting a key goal of the Trump administration's tariff policy.
  • UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) -13% – Shares tumbled after the health insurer slashed its full-year adjusted EPS guidance to $26.00–$26.50, down from $29.50–$30.00 and below the $29.74 consensus, following weaker-than-expected Q1 results. The disappointing update dragged down the broader managed care space, with Cigna (CI) down 3% and CVS Health (CVS) and Humana (HUM) -6%.
  • Global Payments (NYSE:GPN) -15% – Shares plunged after the company announced it will acquire the remaining 55% stake in Worldpay from private equity firm GTCR and the remaining 45% from FIS (FIS) for a total transaction value of $24.25B, including $1.55B in anticipated tax assets. At the same time, GPN is offloading its Issuer Solutions business to FIS for $13.5B, with FIS funding the acquisition through a mix of $8B in new debt and proceeds from the Worldpay sale. The complex deal structure raised investor concerns over execution risk, integration complexity, and elevated leverage, contributing to the sharp selloff in GPN shares. Meanwhile, FIS rose 6%, as the transaction allows it to fully monetize a non-cash-generating asset while gaining a high-margin, recurring revenue business expected to enhance adjusted EBITDA margins, EPS, and cash flow within 12 months, with $125M+ in long-term revenue synergies and $150M+ in EBITDA synergies projected by year three.
